ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers cooked turkey meat broadly; no code specific to roasted breast with skin is available. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roasted turkey breast with skin is approximately 85 g (3 oz), equivalent to two to three slices of breast meat with the skin intact.

nutrient_source.title: Turkey, breast, meat and skin, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 05165)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for turkey breast meat and skin, roasted (NDB No. 05165) is an exact match for this food. Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are preserved from the HPB Singapore input record (confidence 0.95) and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values. Saturated fat (2.10 g), monounsaturated fat (2.49 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.87 g), trans fat (0.0 g), sugar (0.0 g), and all additional micronutrients are sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05165.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g per 100 g); reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted turkey breast with skin contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Pure protein and fat foods are not assigned a glycemic index value in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed GI literature because they produce no measurable postprandial blood glucose response. Glycemic index is set to null (not_applicable) per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. Reference to Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care confirms that animal protein foods with zero carbohydrate are excluded from GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (63 mg/100 g) and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (288 mg K : 63 mg Na)
hypertension.guidance: Roasted turkey breast with skin is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its sodium content of 63 mg/100 g is well within recommended limits, and the potassium content of 288 mg/100 g supports vascular tone — however, patients should avoid brined, pre-seasoned, or deli-style turkey products that may contain substantially higher sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (28.71 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted turkey breast with skin is appropriate for individuals with type 2 diabetes as it contains no carbohydrate or sugar and therefore produces no postprandial glycaemic response; the high protein content supports satiety and lean muscle maintenance, and the modest fat from the skin (7.41 g/100 g, saturated fat 2.10 g) does not adversely affect insulin sensitivity when consumed in typical serving sizes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (7.41 g/100 g) with saturated fat at 2.10 g/100 g; cholesterol 74 mg/100 g; high protein supports lean body mass
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted turkey breast with skin is generally suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; saturated fat at 2.10 g/100 g is well below the 5 g threshold of concern, cholesterol is moderate at 74 mg/100 g, and the high protein-to-fat ratio supports a heart-healthy dietary pattern — removing the skin reduces saturated fat to approximately 0.9 g/100 g for those requiring stricter lipid management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of positive anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, whole-food fibre >3 g); saturated fat 2.10 g/100 g is below threshold but no active anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roasted turkey breast with skin is nutritionally neutral for rheumatoid arthritis — saturated fat is below the 5 g concern threshold and trans fat is absent, but the food provides no positive anti-inflammatory factors; individuals with RA should remove the skin to further reduce saturated fat and pair this food with anti-inflammatory vegetables, oily fish, or omega-3-rich foods.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted turkey breast with skin is a cooked poultry food consisting of the white breast meat of turkey with the skin retained, prepared by dry-heat oven roasting until fully cooked through. The result is a tender, slightly firm, moist white meat with a lightly browned, flavourful skin that contributes additional fat and calories compared to skinless preparations. Per 100 g, it provides 189 kcal, 28.71 g protein, 7.41 g total fat (of which approximately 2.10 g is saturated fat), and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food. Sodium is low at 63 mg/100 g and potassium is 288 mg/100 g. Turkey breast is a lean white meat widely consumed in Western and American cuisine, particularly associated with Thanksgiving and Christmas meals in North America, and is also commonly used in everyday meal preparation as a high-protein, low-carbohydrate protein source. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes (no glycaemic impact), hypertension (low sodium), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat). It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. The skin adds approximately 1.2 g saturated fat per 100 g compared to skinless breast; removing the skin is recommended for individuals requiring strict saturated fat restriction.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from the Singapore HPB database (input record, confidence 0.95) and are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05165 for turkey breast meat and skin, roasted — an exact food match from an approved primary source. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamins, minerals) are drawn directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, an approved secondary global source. No estimation or analogy was required for the primary nutrient profile.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per both HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05165) for roasted turkey breast with skin — naturally zero as this is a pure animal protein and fat food with no carbohydrate-containing ingredients. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05165; confirmed zero for this preparation. Vitamin_c_mg and vitamin_a_mcg_rae are 0.0 per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this food. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 rules for negligible-carbohydrate foods. GI source references Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care as a peer-reviewed journal confirming that pure protein/fat animal foods are excluded from GI classification. Monounsaturated_fat_g (2.49 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.87 g), and all micronutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05165. Halal status is conditionally true assuming standard slaughter practices; kosher status is false as turkey requires specific kosher certification. The rheumatoid_arthritis health context from the input record has been preserved and corrected — the input incorrectly set suitable=false while the reason text described a neutral-to-positive profile; the corrected record sets concern_level='moderate' reflecting the absence of positive anti-inflammatory factors rather than active harm.
